prompt: In an AI-driven SDLC where assistants generate most of the code, where does the delivery bottleneck typically move to?
choices: Nowhere; AI removes all bottlenecks so delivery is uniformly faster
To specifying intent clearly, reviewing for correctness and security, and verifying the output — because generation is no longer the slow part
To typing code faster, since that is still the main constraint
To buying more GPUs so the assistant runs faster
explanation: When code appears in seconds, writing it stops being the constraint. Throughput is then gated by how fast humans can specify precisely, review trustworthily, and verify — so that's where the bottleneck moves, and where a manager should focus.

prompt: A feature looks '90% done in a day' with AI assistance but the last 10% takes a week. What does this teach about estimating an AI-driven project?
choices: That the last 10% should just be skipped to hit the date
That the team is simply too slow and needs to be replaced
That AI estimates are always wrong, so estimation should be abandoned
That generation gets much faster but edge cases, integration, and debugging plausible-but-wrong code don't shrink the same way, so you must estimate the review and verification work explicitly
explanation: AI compresses the 'get something working' part, but edge cases, integration, and debugging confident-but-wrong output remain. Pricing the whole project as uniformly faster ignores the last-10% reality; you estimate the review/integration/verification work separately.

prompt: Which of the following are risks specifically heightened by an AI-driven SDLC? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Architectural drift, as each locally-reasonable suggestion makes the codebase less consistent
Security and IP/licensing exposure from generated code (insecure patterns, echoed or outdated dependencies)
The office coffee machine being broken on Mondays
Automation bias — accepting plausible generated code without genuine review because it looks right
explanation: Over-trusting confident output, security/IP risk in generated code, and architectural drift from unenforced patterns are all amplified when AI writes much of the code. A broken coffee machine isn't a delivery risk for the feature and doesn't belong on the register.

prompt: When AI generates a large share of the code, how should the team's quality gates and definition of done change?
choices: Replace all gates with a single check that the code compiles
Strengthen them — human review of generated code, automated tests, type checks, and security scanning become the gates that make AI speed safe, and 'done' means clearing them
Relax them, since the AI already wrote working code and review just slows everyone down
Define done as the number of lines or PRs the assistant produced
explanation: A fast stream of plausible, confident code raises the stakes on review, so gates get more important, not less. 'Done' shifts from 'it compiles / the demo works' to 'it passed human review, tests, and security/type checks,' with a human accountable for the merge.

prompt: TRADE-OFF: A teammate proposes merging AI-generated code quickly with only a light skim to capture the velocity, instead of doing thorough human review plus tests on every change. What is the trade-off?
choices: Both approaches produce identical quality, so the choice only affects paperwork

Light review ships faster now but lets subtle, confident bugs and insecure patterns through, raising defect-escape and maintenance cost; thorough review is slower but catches what fast, plausible code hides — the right call depends on the stakes of the change
Thorough review is always mandatory and light review can never be used for anything
There is no trade-off; light review is always better because it is faster
explanation: It's a real speed-vs-safety trade-off. Light review captures velocity but AI's plausible, confident output is exactly where subtle bugs and insecure patterns hide, so escapes and rework rise. The right balance depends on stakes — a throwaway prototype can skim; production or regulated code can't.
